title: Points or Spans? A Benchmark-and-Analysis Study of Representative Families in Supervised Contrastive Learning
description: This project is a benchmark-and-analysis study rather than a new method paper. It asks a narrow question: under matched supervised contrastive training, when do low-rank span representatives preserve latent subclass structure better than point representatives?
motivation: Prior work already establishes that supervised contrastive learning can collapse latent subclasses, and prior methods already cover multi-prototype representatives, class-level orthonormal controls, subclass-aware balancing, and coarse-to-fine subclass-preserving objectives. The remaining gap is to isolate representative family choice itself, asking when spans help over points under matched training and under a compute budget small enough to be reproduced on a single GPU.
proposed_approach: Implement a common supervised-contrastive template with the same encoder, optimizer, temperatures, and classifier rule for all methods, and vary only the representative family: SupCon, PSC, MPSC, a CLOP-style class-control baseline, or multiple low-rank class-local spans. For every class y, compute all-class logits with a log-sum-exp over representative scores; use assignment-agreement weights only for same-label positive pairs; and keep span-specific regularization mild so the comparison reflects points versus spans rather than stronger balancing heuristics.

related_work.title: Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.authors: Prannay Khosla, Piotr Teterwak, Chen Wang, Aaron Sarna, Yonglong Tian, Phillip Isola, Aaron Maschinot, Ce Liu, Dilip Krishnan
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides the base SupCon objective but no persistent within-class representative family, so it cannot answer whether multiple points or multiple spans are the better subclass-preserving abstraction.

related_work.title: Contrastive Learning Based Hybrid Networks for Long-Tailed Image Classification
related_work.authors: Peng Wang, Kai Han, Xiu-Shen Wei, Lei Zhang, Lei Wang
related_work.how_it_differs: This is the closest overlap because it introduces PSC and discusses MPSC, but it does not isolate points versus spans under matched supervised-contrastive training. Its main framing is long-tailed hybrid classification and memory-efficient prototypes.

related_work.title: Preventing Collapse in Contrastive Learning with Orthonormal Prototypes
related_work.authors: Huanran Li, Manh Nguyen, Daniel Pimentel-Alarcon
related_work.how_it_differs: Shows that orthonormal prototypes or subspaces can prevent class collapse, but focuses on class-level separation and collapse prevention rather than comparing multi-point and multi-span within-class representatives.

related_work.title: Perfectly Balanced: Improving Transfer and Robustness of Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.authors: Mayee Chen, Daniel Y. Fu, Avanika Narayan, Michael Zhang, Zhao Song, Kayvon Fatahalian, Christopher Re
related_work.how_it_differs: Targets subclass preservation and coarse-to-fine transfer, but changes the objective with weighted class-conditional InfoNCE and an autoencoder. It therefore does not identify whether points or spans are the right representative family.

related_work.title: Subclass-balancing Contrastive Learning for Long-tailed Recognition
related_work.authors: Chengkai Hou, Jieyu Zhang, Haonan Wang, Tianyi Zhou
related_work.how_it_differs: Uses dynamic subclass clustering in a long-tailed setting and therefore changes the task regime and pseudo-label mechanism together. It does not hold the supervised-contrastive template fixed and vary only representative family.

related_work.title: Which Features are Learnt by Contrastive Learning? On the Role of Simplicity Bias in Class Collapse and Feature Suppression
related_work.authors: Yihao Xue, Siddharth Joshi, Eric Gan, Pin-Yu Chen, Baharan Mirzasoleiman
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides theoretical motivation for why supervised contrastive learning can collapse latent subclasses, but does not study explicit representative families.

related_work.title: A Theoretical Framework for Preventing Class Collapse in Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.authors: Chungpa Lee, Jeongheon Oh, Kibok Lee, Jae-yong Sohn
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides a recent theory paper on preventing class collapse, but does not address the specific benchmark question of when low-rank spans help over point representatives.

hypothesis: When latent subclasses differ mainly through a few dominant within-class directions that survive embedding normalization, low-rank span representatives will outperform point representatives on synthetic mode recovery and on coarse-to-fine transfer. When subclass structure is mostly mean-separated or nearly isotropic, the advantage of spans should shrink or vanish.

confirm: On the synthetic directional-low-rank regime, the span family beats MPSC on at least two recovery metrics, including either AMI or principal-angle error.
On the synthetic mean-separated or isotropic regime, the span advantage shrinks materially or disappears.
On CIFAR-100 coarse-to-fine transfer, the span family improves over SupCon by at least 0.5 point on linear-probe or 20-NN accuracy.
On the same transfer metric, the span family is no worse than the better of PSC and MPSC by more than 0.3 point.
On the fine-label sanity check, the span family stays within 0.5 point of SupCon.
The main result does not depend on occupancy forcing, repeated resets, or another strong auxiliary heuristic.
refute: MPSC matches or outperforms the span family in the synthetic directional-low-rank regime and on CIFAR-100 coarse-to-fine transfer.
The claimed span advantage does not change across directional-low-rank and mean-separated or isotropic synthetic regimes.
Span gains appear only when strong balancing or reset heuristics are enabled, changing the paper's benchmark question.
